## Two-Hop Architecture Chain: GPT-3 → GPT-2 → Attention Is All You Need

The chain is anchored in passages from the cited papers themselves, so each hop is independently evident and no direct GPT-3 → Transformer citation is invented.

### Hop 1 — GPT-3 inherits GPT-2's architecture

In §2.1 ("Model and Architectures"), the GPT-3 paper states:

> "We use the same model and architecture as GPT-2 [RWC+19], including the modified initialization, pre-normalization, and reversible tokenization described therein, with the exception that we use alternating dense and locally banded sparse attention patterns in the layers of the transformer, similar to the Sparse Transformer [CGRS19]." [1]

**What this hop establishes:** GPT-3 does not introduce a new architecture; it reuses GPT-2's model wholesale. The single deviation (alternating dense / locally banded sparse attention) is borrowed from the separate Sparse Transformer line [CGRS19], not from any change to the underlying backbone. Crucially, GPT-3 attributes the architecture to GPT-2, not to Vaswani et al. — the architectural lineage flows through GPT-2, not around it.

### Hop 2 — GPT-2's architecture is the Transformer of Vaswani et al.

In §2.3 ("Model"), the GPT-2 paper states:

> "We use a Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) based architecture for our LMs. The model largely follows the details of the OpenAI GPT model (Radford et al., 2018) with a few modifications." [2]

**What this hop establishes:** GPT-2 grounds its backbone explicitly in the Transformer of Vaswani et al. 2017. The same paper's architecture-hyperparameter table (layers, d_model) describes standard Transformer dimensions, confirming the backbone is the Vaswani-et-al. Transformer.

The Transformer itself is introduced in the cited paper as:

> "We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely." [3]

> "In this work we propose the Transformer, a model architecture eschewing recurrence and instead relying entirely on an attention mechanism to draw global dependencies between input and output." [4]

### Chain integrity — no fabricated direct link

Each hop is anchored in the citing paper itself, and the two hops compose cleanly into the chain **GPT-3 → GPT-2 → Attention Is All You Need**:

| Edge | Anchor paper | Anchor passage | What is established |
|------|--------------|----------------|---------------------|
| GPT-3 ← GPT-2 | GPT-3 §2.1 | "same model and architecture as GPT-2" | GPT-3 reuses GPT-2's architecture [1] |
| GPT-2 ← Transformer | GPT-2 §2.3 | "Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) based architecture" | GPT-2 is a Transformer [2] |
| Transformer defined | Attention Is All You Need, Abstract / §1 | "new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms" | The Transformer is introduced here [3] [4] |

No direct GPT-3 → Vaswani et al. architecture citation is asserted. The retrieved citation graph confirms this: GPT-3's only architecture edge is to GPT-2, and GPT-2's architecture edge points to Attention Is All You Need. The connection is mediated entirely through GPT-2 — exactly what the source text supports.

Sources
[1] Language Models are Few-Shot Learners, 2.1 Model and Architectures, p. 8
[2] Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners, 2.3. Model, p. 4
[3] Attention Is All You Need, Abstract, p. 1
[4] Attention Is All You Need, 1 Introduction, p. 2
